Clarification Question:
For many SMEs, the additional expectations around Cyber Essentials Plus and ISO 14001 — on top of existing requirements such as DTAC, DSPT, and broader cybersecurity and regulatory obligations — represent a substantial financial and operational challenge.

I would be grateful if you could clarify the following points:
Could you explain why Class I MHRA products have been excluded from the predictive AI SaMD category? Is there a specific regulatory or strategic rationale behind this decision?

award onto the NHS SBS framework itself, or
a subsequent contract award from an individual NHS trust or customer organisation?
I also wanted to share a broader concern that the increasing number of overlapping regulatory and compliance requirements can feel disproportionate for smaller innovators, particularly when the associated costs are significant and the incremental impact on real-world patient safety may not always be clear.

However, I noted that quality criteria appear to account for only 10% of the overall weighting, and I wondered whether this balance may unintentionally disadvantage clinically driven innovation.
NHS SBS Response:
Dear Bidder,

This framework relates to Healthcare AI Solutions and is primarily intended for use within the NHS. As such, compliance with recognised clinical standards is essential to ensure both clinical integrity and patient safety. The standards referenced have undergone external governance review and represent the minimum requirements mandated under this framework.

Given the nature of the technology being procured through this framework, it is imperative that solutions comply with robust clinical standards. These requirements align not only with the Authority’s expectations but also with NHS England and wider UK Government policies and standards to ensure the security, resilience, and protection of sensitive health data.

We confirm that all required certifications must be in place at the point of award to the framework.

We would also like to highlight that this framework is open to a wide range of suppliers, including SMEs and larger organisations. There are no minimum organisational size thresholds for participation; however, all bidders must demonstrate the capability to meet the specified requirements.

Additionally, please note that the Quality weighting is set at 90% for Lots 1–6 and 70% for Lot 7.

Kind Regards
NHS SBS

Clarification Question:
Modular deployment options Section 5.6.2 states the solution must offer modular deployment options including cloud, hybrid, or on-premise. Where a supplier delivers a UK/EEA-hosted SaaS solution that meets all data residency, security, and assurance requirements, is cloud-only deployment acceptable, or is on-premise availability mandatory for Lot 6 eligibility?


ESR and Allocate integration Section 5.6.2 requires the solution to integrate seamlessly with ERP and workforce management systems such as ESR and Allocate. Please clarify whether this means native API integration, or whether solutions that operate on top of these systems (for example, a browser-based overlay or guidance layer running within the ESR or Allocate user interface) satisfy the requirement.


Mobile-friendly user interfaces Section 5.6.2 states the solution must include mobile-friendly user interfaces. Please confirm whether responsive web design meets this requirement, or whether a native mobile application is required.
NHS SBS Response:
Dear Bidder,

NHS SBS can provide the following clarifications in response to your queries:

Modular Deployment Options (Section 5.6.2):
A solution is accepted if that the solution meets all relevant data residency, security, and assurance requirements outlined within the specification. On-premise deployment is not mandatory for Lot 6 eligibility; however, suppliers should demonstrate sufficient flexibility to meet varying customer requirements where applicable.

ESR and Allocate Integration (Section 5.6.2):
The requirement for integration with ERP and workforce management systems, such as ESR and Allocate, is intended to ensure interoperability and seamless data exchange. Native API integration is one acceptable approach; however, alternative methods (such as solutions operating as a browser-based overlay or guidance layer within existing system interfaces) may also satisfy the requirement, provided they enable effective and secure integration and do not compromise system performance or user experience.

Mobile-Friendly User Interfaces (Section 5.6.2):
Mobile-friendly user interfaces may be delivered through responsive web design. A native mobile application is not a mandatory requirement, provided that the solution is fully accessible, functional, and user-friendly across mobile devices.

Bidders should ensure that their submissions clearly demonstrate how their solution meets these requirements in practice.

Please refer to 2.2 Healthcare AI Solutions Specification for detailed requirements. Approved Organisations may also have specific requirements relating to deployment, integration, and user interfaces, which will be determined on a case-by-case basis during the call-off stage.

Kind Regards,
NHS SBS

Clarification Question:
Algorithmic Transparency Standard timing Section 2.8 requires suppliers to support Approved Organisations in adhering to the Algorithmic Transparency Standard. Please confirm whether suppliers are required to have published an Algorithmic Transparency Recording Standard (ATRS) entry as a condition of award, or whether this is a call-off stage requirement.


ICO review of HR algorithms Section 5.6.3 states that any algorithms used within solutions for HR applications must have been reviewed by the ICO. Please confirm whether this applies to solutions that support HR-related processes (such as training, onboarding, or workforce enablement) but do not make or influence HR decisions about individuals.


NHS Blueprints applicability Section 5.6.3 requires adherence to NHS-approved architectural standards (Blueprints). Please confirm which specific Blueprints apply to Lot 6 Operational Efficiency solutions and where suppliers can access the current versions.
NHS SBS Response:
Dear Bidder,

NHS SBS can provide the following clarifications in response to your queries:

Algorithmic Transparency Standard (Section 2.8):
Suppliers are not required to have published an Algorithmic Transparency Recording Standard (ATRS) entry as a condition of framework award. However, suppliers must be able to support Approved Organisations in adhering to the requirements of the Algorithmic Transparency Standard at the call-off stage and during contract delivery.

ICO Review of HR Algorithms (Section 5.6.3):
The requirement for ICO review applies to algorithms that directly inform or influence HR decisions about individuals. Solutions that support HR-related processes (such as training, onboarding, or workforce enablement), but do not make or materially influence decisions about individuals, would not typically fall within this requirement. However, suppliers should ensure compliance where their solution functionality extends into decision-making or profiling activities.

NHS Blueprints Applicability (Section 5.6.3):
Suppliers are expected to align with relevant NHS-approved architectural standards (Blueprints) where applicable to their solution. As specific Blueprints may vary depending on the nature and deployment of the solution, suppliers should reference the latest guidance and available standards via NHS England digital and architecture resources. Suppliers are responsible for ensuring ongoing compliance with any applicable and updated standards at the point of call-off.

Kind Regards,
NHS SBS

Clarification Question: Can you confirm whether a robotic system that performs all or part of a process, such as medication dispensing or managing a patient and/or care pathway, is considered a medical device? If so, should we assume that these robotic system(s) will require regulatory approval? Please confirm whether this is correct.
NHS SBS Response:
Dear Bidder,

Yes, a robotic system that performs all or part of a clinical process—such as medication dispensing or managing patient care pathways—is considered a medical device where it has a defined medical purpose. In such cases, the system will require appropriate regulatory approval, including CE marking (EU MDR) or UKCA marking (UK regulations), as applicable.

A robotic system is likely to be classified as a medical device where it:

- Performs or supports clinical functions related to diagnosis, treatment, or monitoring
- Influences clinical decision-making or patient outcomes
- Acts on patient-specific data for medical purposes (e.g., dosing, treatment pathways)

However, a robotic system would not typically be classified as a medical device if:

- It is purely administrative (e.g., scheduling, logistics, inventory management)
- It does not influence clinical decisions
- It does not act on patient-specific data for medical purposes

Bidders should clearly justify the classification of their system, and where a system meets the definition of a medical device, provide evidence of valid regulatory approval as part of due diligence.

Kind Regards,
NHS SBS

Clarification Question:
1. Product Changes
(Call-Off Contract, Schedule 2, Clauses 10.1.13 & 10.1.19)
The contract states that bidders cannot make any "significant modification" to goods or services without prior written consent from the NHS trust, and that quality control process changes require 21 days written notice. "Significant" is not defined in the contract.

Can we please get clarity on what constitutes a "significant modification" and what falls under a "quality control process change"? 

2. IP Licence Scope
(Call-Off Contract, Schedule 2, Clause 11 / Framework Agreement, Clause 20.4)
As a default, the contract grants the Approved Organisation an irrevocable, royalty-free, perpetual licence to Intellectual Property Rights, Services Information, and Product Information.

We would like clarity on the intended scope of the IP licence. As drafted, the language appears broad enough to capture our underlying AI model, prompts, configurations, and deployment methodology, rather than just the outputs produced for that trust. We would like to confirm whether the intention is for the licence to cover only deliverables and outputs produced during the engagement, with the underlying platform, models, and proprietary IP expressly excluded.

3. Data Protection and Sub-Processor Authorisation
(Data Protection Protocol, Clause 1.6.7)
The contract requires prior specific written authorisation from the Approved Organisation for every sub-processor, with 14 days notice before any new sub-processor is onboarded.

Can we get clarity on both the scope and the process for sub-processor authorisation? On scope, is the intention that this covers all sub-processors across our technical stack, or only those that directly handle patient data (PHI)? On process, is there a standard Framework-level approved sub-processor list for common infrastructure providers such as AWS, Azure, or GCP that applies across all call-offs, or does each individual trust need to separately authorise the same providers from the beginning?

4. Tender Process
We have two related questions on how the tender process works in practice:
We understand the contract only becomes binding on joint signature of the Order Form. When is the Specification and Tender Response Document finalised and locked in? Is there a clarification or negotiation window between submitting a tender response and the Order Form being executed, or does the submission itself fix our commitments?
The Order Form states that Special Terms in Section H may only be proposed by the Approved Organisation. What route, if any, is available to us to raise concerns or propose protective provisions before an Order Form is signed? Is there a formal channel for this within either the further competition or direct award process?
NHS SBS Response:
Dear Bidder,

1. The call off contract is between the supplier and approved organisation. The interpretation of what constitutes a “significant modification” or a “quality control process change” is therefore intended to be determined at Call-Off stage, taking into account the specific scope and nature of the services being delivered.

2. Approved Organisations have the rights to use, access, and benefit from the deliverables and outputs generated through the provision of the Services. Suppliers will retain ownership of their background Intellectual Property, including their core platforms, models, and proprietary technology.

3. The scope and application of sub-processor requirements will depend on the specific data protection and processing arrangements agreed at Call-Off stage with the Approved Organisation. We confirm there is no standard Framework-level approved sub-processor list. Each Approved Organisation is responsible for reviewing and authorising sub-processors relevant to its specific Call-Off Contract. Suppliers should therefore expect to provide transparency regarding their sub-processing arrangements and agree these with each Approved Organisation.

4. The requirements of the Healthcare AI Solutions framework are defined in the specification document 2.2 Healthcare AI Solutions Specification. This document is intentionally high level to accommodate a wide range of AI solutions. Suppliers are required to demonstrate compliance with these requirements during the tender process and achieve the minimum evaluation threshold to be appointed to the Framework. 
Detailed and specific requirements are expected to be refined at Call-Off stage between the Supplier and the Approved Organisation. This is done through the Order Form and associated schedules, which together form the binding contract once executed.
There is typically an opportunity for dialogue during further competition or engagement prior to execution of the Order Form, allowing both parties to clarify and mutually agree requirements. While Special Terms in Section H are formally issued by the Approved Organisation, suppliers may raise concerns or seek clarifications pre-award, which the Approved Organisation may consider and incorporate where appropriate before finalising the Order Form.

Kind Regards

Clarification Question: Can the Authority clarify what level of FHIR compliance is expected for solutions within Lot 6, for example, whether solutions are required to expose FHIR-compliant APIs for data exchange, whether it is sufficient to be able to consume and process content from FHIR-compliant sources, whether agentic AI capabilities that interact with FHIR-compliant systems require a different or higher level of compliance, or whether the level required may vary depending on the specific use case and integrations defined at call-off stage?
NHS SBS Response:
Dear Bidder,

The Authority expects suppliers under Lot 6 to demonstrate the capability to support interoperability with FHIR standards where relevant; as a minimum, solutions should be able to consume, process, and exchange data from FHIR-compliant sources. 

While exposing FHIR-compliant APIs is not a mandatory requirement at framework level, it is considered best practice where solutions are designed to integrate with NHS systems and may be required depending on the integration needs of a given use case. 

The inclusion of agentic AI capabilities does not in itself create a higher baseline requirement; however, suppliers must ensure that any interaction with FHIR-based systems maintains data integrity, interoperability, and clinical safety. 

The specific level of FHIR compliance—such as API exposure, resource creation, or full conformance with particular FHIR profiles—will be defined and assessed at call-off stage, based on the requirements of the Approved Organisation and the intended use case.

Kind Regards,
NHS SBS

Clarification Question:

Where a solution includes agentic AI capabilities that may interact with or produce outputs that reference SNOMED-coded systems, does this alter the level of SNOMED CT compliance expected, or is the applicable level of compliance determined at call-off stage based on the specific use case defined by each Approved Organisation?
NHS SBS Response:
Dear Bidder,

For solutions integrating multiple data sources that include SNOMED CT or coded clinical content, the Authority expects suppliers to demonstrate the capability, as a minimum, to accurately process, interpret, retrieve, and preserve SNOMED-coded data where it exists. 

Mandatory generation or encoding of SNOMED CT content is not universally required at framework level; however, solutions should be able to support SNOMED CT use where this is relevant to the use case. Where agentic AI capabilities are included, this does not in itself change the baseline expectation, but suppliers must ensure that any outputs interacting with or referencing SNOMED-coded systems maintain semantic accuracy, clinical safety, and interoperability. 

The specific level of SNOMED CT compliance—such as active coding, mapping, or terminology services integration—will be determined at call-off stage by Approved Organisations, based on the defined clinical and operational use case.

Kind Regards,
NHS SBS

Market Alignment & Evidence: Our workforce solutions are currently utilized by 58% of all NHS Trusts. Across our entire footprint, not a single NHS Trust has ever requested, required, or utilised SNOMED CT coding for workforce management or operational staffing systems, as these systems do not process clinical data or patient records.
Proportionality and Market Restriction: Applying a clinical data standard as a mandatory hurdle for non-clinical software creates an artificial barrier to entry. This risks unintentionally excluding market-leading, innovative AI solutions that the NHS desperately needs to tackle its current operational and backlog challenges.
Impact on Framework Goals: A stock response indicating this is required simply due to an "NHS focus" overlooks the critical distinction between clinical NHS software and operational NHS software. Maintaining this as a blanket mandate for all lots will significantly reduce competition and limit the scope of Lot 6.


Suggested Resolution: Would the Authority consider amending this pre-requisite to be conditional based on the Lot or system type? Specifically, making SNOMED CT compliance mandatory only for solutions that process clinical data or direct patient records, and allowing a "Not Applicable" justification for purely operational, non-clinical workforce solutions under Lot 6?
NHS SBS Response:
Dear Bidder,

This framework relates to Healthcare AI Solutions and is primarily intended for use within the NHS. As such, compliance with recognised clinical standards is essential to ensure both clinical integrity and patient safety. The standards referenced have undergone external governance review and represent the minimum requirements mandated under this framework.

Given the nature of the technology being procured through this framework, it is imperative that solutions comply with robust clinical standards. These requirements align not only with the Authority’s expectations but also with NHS England and wider UK Government policies and standards to ensure the security, resilience, and protection of sensitive health data.

Kind Regards,
NHS SBS

Clarification Question:
The current pass/fail criteria function as mandatory Conditions of Participation, where failure to meet any single requirement results in automatic exclusion from the Framework without further evaluation.
While we recognize the need for baseline compliance, the structure of these criteria may unintentionally prevent otherwise highly qualified and capable suppliers from participating—particularly where requirements are binary (Yes/No) and do not accommodate equivalent controls, phased compliance, or clarifications.
This approach risks excluding solutions that could deliver significant clinical and operational value, even where any identified gaps are minor, transitional, or remediable prior to service commencement.
We respectfully suggest that certain criteria be assessed on a scored or conditional basis, or that evidence of equivalent controls or planned compliance be accepted, to ensure the Framework promotes fair competition and selects the most advantageous solutions for the NHS.
Specifically, (points 1-9)
1. With respect to Prerequisite 3.2.2, “If your solution is classed as a medical device, please confirm you are CE Mark certified, “could this requirement be clarified or revised to better align with referential clinical decision support tools? Our solution provides evidence-based reference content and does not autonomously make or execute clinical decisions. Accordingly, it is not classified as a medical device. Selecting “No” may not appropriately represent our capabilities and could result in unintended exclusion from consideration. We would appreciate clarification on how best to address this requirement for solutions of this type.
2. Regarding Prerequisite 3.2.3, Does your solution meet the NHS Digital Assessment Criteria (DTAC)?, our solution is a reference-only resource and does not process, store, or transmit patient-identifiable data. Therefore, it aligns with relevant NHS Digital Assessment Criteria (DTAC) requirements where applicable. However, certain DTAC controls may not apply due to the solution’s non–patient data processing functionality. We would appreciate clarification on how best to address this requirement for solutions of this type.
3. Regarding Prerequisite 3.2.4, “Does your solution support interoperability through the use of FHIR standards?,” our solution does not exchange or integrate data with external systems. As such, interoperability through FHIR standards is not applicable. Please confirm whether this response satisfies the requirement.”
4. Regarding Prerequisite, 3.2.5, “Does your solution support and implement SNOMED clinical terminology in accordance with NHS requirements?”, our solution does not implement SNOMED CT clinical terminology in the manner required by NHS standards, as it does not function as a system of record or a transactional clinical system.
Our solution is a read-only, evidence-based clinical decision support solution that provides synthesized clinical guidance to support clinician decision-making. It does not create, store, or exchange patient data and therefore does not perform terminology management activities such as SNOMED CT code authoring, validation, mapping, or structured data exchange.
While our product supports SNOMED CT and other standard coding systems to enhance search functionality and enable context-sensitive linking from external systems, this support is limited to facilitating content retrieval rather than implementing or enforcing clinical terminology standards.
Accordingly, the requirement to “support and implement SNOMED clinical terminology in accordance with NHS requirements” is considered not applicable to our solution. We would appreciate clarification on how best to address this requirement for solutions of this type.
5. Regarding Prerequisite 3.2.6, “Is your solution WCAG 2.2 AA level compliant?,” Our solution is currently progressing toward full compliance with WCAG 2.2 Level AA standards. We would like to confirm whether suppliers actively working toward compliance (supported by a defined roadmap and ongoing accessibility improvements) remain eligible to participate in this Framework.
6. Regarding Prerequisite 3.3.1, “please confirm if your organisation is compliant with the MHRA Guidance,” our solution is not classified as a medical device under the current MHRA guidance and therefore is not subject to UKCA marking requirements. The solution functions as an evidence-based clinical reference resource and aligns with MHRA principles for non-device software, as it does not process patient-specific data or provide automated diagnostic or treatment decisions. We would appreciate clarification on how best to address this requirement for solutions of this type.
7. Regarding Prerequisite 3.3.2, “Please confirm if your organisation is compliant with the DCB0129 Clinical Risk Management standards,” our solution is an evidence-based clinical decision support reference tool. It does not independently process patient data, generate patient-specific outputs, or execute clinical workflows.
As such, it is generally not considered a manufacturer-regulated health IT system within the scope of DCB0129. We therefore believe this requirement may not be directly applicable to solutions of this type.
We would welcome guidance on how best to address this requirement in the context of reference-only clinical decision support solutions.
8. Regarding Prerequisite 3.3.3, “Please confirm your organisation is compliant with DSP Toolkit or working towards it.,” our solution is an evidence-based clinical decision support reference solution that does not store or process patient-identifiable data on behalf of customers. As a result, we are generally not required to complete the DSP Toolkit as a data controller or data processor within NHS environments. Responsibility for DSP Toolkit compliance typically rests with the deploying NHS organization that integrates and uses the solution within its clinical systems and workflows. We would appreciate clarification on how best to address this requirement for solutions of this type.
9. Can we include a qualifying clarification to any prerequisite requirement?
NHS SBS Response:
Dear Bidder,

This framework relates to Healthcare AI Solutions and is primarily intended for use within the NHS. As such, compliance with recognised clinical standards is essential to ensure both clinical integrity and patient safety. The standards referenced have undergone external governance review and represent the minimum requirements mandated under this framework.

Given the nature of the technology being procured through this framework, it is imperative that solutions comply with robust clinical standards. These requirements align not only with the Authority’s expectations but also with NHS England and wider UK Government policies and standards to ensure the security, resilience, and protection of sensitive health data.

We confirm that all required certifications and mandatory requirements must be in place to become an awarded supplier on the framework agreement.

Kind Regards,
NHS SBS

Clarification Question:
We are reviewing the tender documentation for SBS10523 (Healthcare AI Solutions) and wish to raise a clarification question regarding the medical device classification requirement.

The relevant clause states that, where a supplier's solution is classified as a medical device (CE Mark or UKCA/UKNI Mark, or successor), it must meet a minimum classification of Class IIa in accordance with UK MDR and MHRA requirements.

We believe this requirement may be based on an incorrect reading of the current UK regulatory position, for the following reasons:

There is no "Class IIa floor" for software in current UK law. Classification under the UK Medical Devices Regulations 2002 (Schedule 2A, derived from the former EU MDD framework) does not contain an equivalent to Rule 11 of EU MDR 2017/745. It is Rule 11 — which does not apply in Great Britain — that de facto up-classifies most clinical decision-support software to Class IIa. Under the UK MDR 2002 as currently in force, a substantial proportion of Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) and AI as a Medical Device (AIaMD) is legitimately classified as Class I and self-certified by the manufacturer.
The MHRA register reflects this. Many lawfully UKCA-marked and CE-marked AIaMD/SaMD products placed on the UK market are registered with the MHRA as Class I devices. A blanket minimum of Class IIa would therefore exclude products that are fully compliant with UK MDR and MHRA requirements, which appears contrary to the clause's own stated basis ("in accordance with UK MDR and MHRA requirements").
The reformed UK framework is not yet in force. The MHRA's forthcoming software/AI regulatory reforms (informed by the AI Airlock pilot and the Software and AI as a Medical Device Change Programme) are anticipated during 2026 but have not yet been enacted. The tender should be assessed against the regulations currently in force, under which Class I remains a valid and common classification for AI solutions in scope.
We would be grateful if you could confirm whether the intention is to:

Exclude all Class I medical devices, in which case we would respectfully suggest this conflicts with the current UK MDR position and may disproportionately restrict competition; or
Require that any device is appropriately classified and conformity-assessed for its actual risk class (i.e. correctly registered with the MHRA at whatever class its intended purpose dictates, with Class IIa+ devices demonstrating Approved Body conformity assessment at Call-Off).
If the latter is intended, we would suggest the clause be amended to require evidence of correct classification and valid registration appropriate to the device's risk class, rather than imposing a minimum of Class IIa.

We would welcome your confirmation so that suppliers offering compliant Class I solutions are not inadvertently excluded.
NHS SBS Response:
Dear Bidder,

This framework relates to Healthcare AI Solutions and is primarily intended for use within the NHS. As such, compliance with recognised clinical standards is essential to ensure both clinical integrity and patient safety. The standards referenced have undergone external governance review and represent the minimum requirements mandated under this framework.

Given the nature of the technology being procured through this framework, it is imperative that solutions comply with robust clinical standards. These requirements align not only with the Authority’s expectations but also with NHS England and wider UK Government policies and standards to ensure the security, resilience, and protection of sensitive health data.

We confirm that all required certifications and mandatory requirements must be in place to become an awarded supplier on the framework agreement, including Class IIa Medical Device.

Kind Regards,
NHS SBS
